examples.title: Simple Reflex Agent - Vacuum Cleaner
examples.description: Implements a simple reflex agent for vacuum cleaner world using condition-action rules
examples.code:
// Simple Reflex Agent for Vacuum Cleaner
FUNCTION SimpleReflexVacuum(percept) RETURNS action
 LOCAL STATIC: rules = set of condition-action rules
 
 state <- InterpretInput(percept)
 rule <- RULE-MATCH(state, rules)
 action <- rule.ACTION
 RETURN action

// Rule Set Example
RULES = [
 {IF state = [Dirty, LocationA] THEN action = Suck},
 {IF state = [Dirty, LocationB] THEN action = Suck},
 {IF state = [Clean, LocationA] THEN action = MoveRight},
 {IF state = [Clean, LocationB] THEN action = MoveLeft}
]
examples.explanation: This simple reflex agent uses condition-action rules (if-then statements) to map percepts directly to actions. It has no internal state memory and only reacts to current perception.

examples.title: Model-Based Reflex Agent
examples.description: Demonstrates a model-based reflex agent that maintains internal world state
examples.explanation: Model-based agents maintain internal state (world model) that gets updated based on percepts and actions taken. This allows handling partially observable environments.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) state update, O(n) rule matching

examples.title: Goal-Based Agent - Navigation
examples.description: A goal-based agent that plans actions to reach destination
examples.explanation: Goal-based agents consider future consequences by formulating goals and finding sequences of actions to achieve those goals.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^d) where b=branching, d=depth to goal

examples.title: Utility-Based Agent
examples.description: Demonstrates utility-based decision making with utility function
examples.code:
// Utility-Based Agent
FUNCTION UtilityBasedAgent(percept) RETURNS action
 LOCAL STATIC: state, utilityFunction
 
 state <- UPDATE-STATE(state, percept)
 
 // Evaluate all possible actions
 actions <- POSSIBLE-ACTIONS(state)
 
 bestAction <- None
 bestUtility <- -infinity
 
 FOR EACH action IN actions
 // Consider all possible outcomes
 outcomes <- GENERATE-OUTCOMES(state, action)
 expectedUtility <- 0
 FOR EACH outcome IN outcomes
 prob <- outcome.probability
 utility <- UTILITY(outcome.state, utilityFunction)
 expectedUtility <- expectedUtility + (prob * utility)
 
 IF expectedUtility > bestUtility THEN
 bestUtility <- expectedUtility
 bestAction <- action
 
 RETURN bestAction

// Utility Function Example
FUNCTION UTILITY(state, utilityFunction)
 RETURN (state.safety * 0.5) + (state.speed * 0.3) + (state.comfort * 0.2)
examples.explanation: Utility-based agents use a utility function to assign numerical values to different states, allowing optimization among multiple competing goals.

examples.title: Learning Agent Architecture
examples.description: Shows the complete learning agent with critic and feedback loop
examples.explanation: Learning agents have four components: performance element (acts), critic (evaluates), learning element (improves), and problem generator (suggests new experiences).
